## SECTION 1 — Global Pulse (Geopolitics & World Economy)
Global economic growth forecasts for 2026 have been revised downwards to approximately 2.5% by the World Bank, amidst rising geopolitical tensions, inflationary pressures, and disruptions in energy markets. Developments in the Middle East continue to significantly influence global markets and energy prices. Despite these global challenges, countries such as India are demonstrating strong growth momentum, driven by technology, infrastructure investment, and domestic consumption. ## SECTION 2 — Global News (Complete Continental Coverage)
### Americas
The 2026 FIFA World Cup is currently underway in North America, having commenced on June 11 and scheduled to conclude on July 19. Historically, June 25, 1993, marked a significant moment as Canada elected its first female head of government, Kim Campbell.
### Europe
A confirmed case of Ebola, originating from the Democratic Republic of Congo, has been identified in France. The World Health Organization (WHO) chief stated on Wednesday that the global risk from this outbreak "remains low".
### Asia-Pacific
The ongoing space race in the Asia-Pacific region sees key players like China and India making significant advancements in their space programs. China successfully launched three taikonauts to its Tiangong space station in May, with plans for a crewed mission to the Moon by the end of the decade and a permanent lunar base by 2035. India is also advancing its lunar exploration with plans for Chandrayaan-4 by 2028 and a collaborative Chandrayaan-5 mission with Japan to explore the lunar South Pole.
### Africa
The Directorate of Criminal Investigations (DCI) in Kenya has issued a security alert ahead of planned Gen Z protests on June 25, commemorating events from 2024 and 2025. Activists, however, insist that these demonstrations will proceed as scheduled despite official warnings. The US Embassy in Kenya also cautioned its citizens about potential traffic disruptions and roadblocks during the anticipated protests.

## SECTION 13 — India National (Politics & Governance)
India criticized Pakistan at the United Nations, reiterating its stance that Jammu and Kashmir "always been, is and will remain" an internal matter. An 'Orange' alert was issued for Mumbai due to heavy overnight monsoon rainfall, which has led to significant waterlogging in several areas. The West Bengal government is set to introduce new legislation to facilitate the auctioning of properties belonging to individuals implicated in corruption cases. Maharashtra is considering a mandate to include birth dates on wedding cards as a measure to curb child marriage in the state. A London court has ordered Nirav Modi to pay ₹100 crore to the Bank of India as part of a loan recovery case. President Droupadi Murmu is scheduled to present the Padma Awards during the second round of the civil investiture ceremony at Rashtrapati Bhavan. The National Highways Authority of India (NHAI) is stepping up efforts against encroachments and unauthorized parking along National Highways. Prime Minister Narendra Modi received a phone call from Armenian Prime Minister Nikol Pashinyan, where Modi thanked him for assisting in the evacuation of Indian nationals stranded in Iran. An Air India plane briefly entered Pakistan airspace while performing a go-around maneuver at Amritsar airport, prompting the aviation regulator DGCA to take interim action against an air traffic controller and the airline's operating crew for not reporting the incident. Congress President Mallikarjun Kharge criticized Union Education Minister Dharmendra Pradhan for allegedly referring to protesting students as "terrorists" and announced that Congress would hold press conferences across 28 cities on June 25 under the banner of 'ChhatronKiGoonj'.

## SECTION 14 — Telangana State Updates
The Special Intensive Revision (SIR) of electoral rolls is set to commence across Telangana from June 25 and will continue until July 24, with a primary objective to purify electoral rolls and ensure all eligible citizens are included. Telangana Chief Minister A. Revanth Reddy has urged ministers and Congress leaders to be vigilant during the SIR process, criticizing any negligent attitude towards the exercise. In Hyderabad, 4,062 Booth Level Officers will conduct door-to-door verification for the SIR across the 15 Assembly constituencies. Telangana Chief Minister A. Revanth Reddy is scheduled to participate in the inauguration of the newly installed crest gates of the Tungabhadra Project on June 25, alongside the Chief Ministers of Andhra Pradesh and Karnataka and the Union Jal Shakti Minister. The BRS party has urged the Election Commission of India (ECI) to implement a "one person one vote" policy during the ongoing Special Intensive Revision of electoral rolls.

---

## SECTION 15 — Andhra Pradesh State Updates
Andhra Pradesh Chief Minister N. Chandrababu Naidu will inaugurate the newly installed crest gates of the Tungabhadra Project in Hosapete, Karnataka, on June 25. The Tungabhadra Project underwent a significant ₹51-crore restoration, including the installation of 33 new spillway gates, enhancing dam safety and securing irrigation and drinking water supply to Andhra Pradesh, Karnataka, and Telangana. This project is vital for Andhra Pradesh, providing irrigation water to approximately 1.46 lakh hectares, particularly in the Kurnool, Kadapa, and Anantapur districts. The Andhra Pradesh government has revised the Muharram school holiday schedule for 2026, with schools now closing on June 26 instead of June 25, to align with updated lunar calendar assessments. Anantapur Police have increased security measures in anticipation of President Droupadi Murmu's visit to the Central University of Andhra Pradesh on July 1 for its first convocation ceremony.

## SECTION 17 — Social Media & Public Sentiment Trends
In Kenya, the anniversary of the 2024 Gen Z protests on June 25 has become a focal point for public sentiment, with activists mobilizing online using hashtags such as #HakiSasa and #JusticeNow. Organizers are actively encouraging participants in the planned demonstrations to record and livestream events on social media, and to utilize hidden cameras to document any instances of excessive force by police. This reflects a strategic use of digital platforms to shape narratives and ensure accountability during public gatherings.

## SECTION 18 — Deep Analysis of Most Important Breaking News
**What Happened:** Kenyan activists are insisting on nationwide demonstrations today, June 25, 2026, to commemorate victims of the 2024 and 2025 Gen Z protests, despite warnings from the Directorate of Criminal Investigations and the US Embassy. Security agencies have stated they are prepared to deal with any chaos, while organizers emphasize peaceful intent.
**Root Causes:** These protests stem from deeply rooted public grievances, including the high cost of living, punitive taxation (as highlighted by the 2024 anti-Finance Bill protests), instances of police violence, and persistent demands for accountability for past killings and repression. Millions of young people face unemployment or underemployment, contributing to widespread discontent.
**Historical Background:** June 25 marks the anniversary of the 2024 anti-finance bill protests, a significant youth-led movement that resulted in widespread unrest and several fatalities, establishing the date as a day of remembrance for those who died. The ongoing mobilization reflects a sustained effort by youth to challenge governmental policies and demand reforms.
**Key Actors:** Key actors include Gen Z activists, opposition leaders such as Martha Karua, James Orengo, and Boniface Mwangi, as well as the Directorate of Criminal Investigations (DCI) and the US Embassy in Kenya, which has issued security advisories. The government has pledged to protect peaceful demonstrators but warned against violence.
**Strategic Importance:** The recurring nature of these protests underscores profound social and economic discontent within Kenya and highlights ongoing challenges related to governance and freedom of assembly. The government's response to these demonstrations, alongside the activists' ability to mobilize, reflects the evolving power dynamics and the crucial role of youth activism in shaping Kenya's political landscape.
**Possible Long-Term Consequences:** Sustained unrest could impact Kenya's national stability and its international standing, potentially leading to further debates on civil liberties or shifts in government policy. The consistent mobilization of youth could also signify a long-term shift in political engagement and influence within the country.

## SECTION 19 — This Day in History (Global)
*   In 1993, both Canada and Turkey elected their first female heads of government, with Kim Campbell in Canada and Tansu Çiller in Turkey.
*   The world's first live global satellite TV program, "Our World," was aired by the BBC in 1967, featuring artists from 19 countries.
*   On June 25, 1950, the Korean War began with the invasion of South Korea by North Korea.
*   "The Diary of Anne Frank" was first published in 1947, becoming a widely recognized work of world literature.
*   Mozambique achieved independence from Portugal in 1975, ending 477 years of Portuguese rule.
*   In 1973, White House Counsel John Dean began testifying before the Senate Watergate Committee, implicating President Richard Nixon in the Watergate cover-up.
*   The U.S. Supreme Court banned official prayers in public schools in 1962.
*   In 1991, the Yugoslav republics of Slovenia and Croatia declared their independence from Yugoslavia, marking the beginning of the breakup of Yugoslavia.
*   The Russian space station Mir was hit by an unmanned cargo vessel in 1997.
*   Microsoft's "Windows 98" operating system was released to the public in 1998.

---

## SECTION 20 — This Day in History (India)
*   On June 25, 1975, then-Prime Minister Indira Gandhi declared a state of internal Emergency across India, which lasted until March 21, 1977. This period is widely regarded as a dark phase in independent India's history due to the suspension of civil liberties, censorship of the press, and widespread arrests of opposition leaders.
*   India lifted its first Cricket World Cup trophy on June 25, 1983, defeating West Indies in the final match at Old Trafford.
